coredata.dc:title: Efficient photocatalytic toluene degradation over heterojunction of GQDs@BiOCl ultrathin nanosheets with selective benzoic acid activation
coredata.dc:creator: Zhao, Xiaoli
Deng, Bangwei
Li, Fei
Huang, Ming
Sun, Yanjuan
Li, Jieyuan
Dong, Fan
coredata.dc:description: Photocatalytic toluene degradation has attracted tremendous attention because of the growing environmental problem. However, conventional photocatalytic materials used for toluene degradation usually suffer from low carrier separation efficiency and poor stability which will degrade the catalytic performance. Herein, we report the synthesis of a novel heterostructure of GQDs@BiOCl ultrathin nanosheets where the GQDs can rapidly capture and transport photogenerated electrons for effective charge separation, promoting the generation of more reactive oxygen species (·O2 - and ·OH radicals) for toluene degradation. In situ DRIFTS measurement and theoretical calculation are performed to unveil the reaction intermediates and the underlying toluene oxidation mechanism. The GQDs@BiOCl heterojunction could facilitate the adsorption and conversion of toluene and the reaction intermediates. Especially, the heterojunction greatly enhances the activation and conversion of benzoic acid and thus expedites the complete toluene degradation. This work presents a new insight on the design of high-performance photocatalysts for efficient degradation of typical air pollutants.
coredata.dcterms:subject: GQDs@BiOCl
Heterojunction
Carrier separation
Photocatalysis
Toluene degradation

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant Nos. 21822601 , 22006009 ), Sichuan Natural Science Foundation for Distinguished Young Scholars ( 2021JDJQ0006 ), and the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities ( ZYGX2019Z021 ), the 111 Project ( B20030 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: SEM images of (a) BiOCl nanosheets and (b) GQDs@BiOCl heterojunction. (c, d) XRD patterns and XPS spectra of BiOCl nanosheets and GQDs@BiOCl heterojunction, respectively.
(a−d) TEM images and the corresponding SAED pattern of BiOCl nanosheet. (e−h) TEM images of the GQDs@BiOCl heterojunction.
(a−d) UV–vis DRS spectra, estimated band gaps, PL spectra, and ns-level time-resolved fluorescence spectra of BiOCl nanosheets and GQDs@BiOCl heterojunction, respectively.

(a) Solid-state EPR spectra of BOCl and GQDs@BiOCl nanosheet under dark and UV-light irradiation for 5 and 15 mins. (b) The difference of charge density distribution of the GQDs@BiOCl heterojunction. Charge accumulation is in blue and depletion is in yellow. The isosurface is set to 0.0004 eV Å−3.

(a, d) DMPO EPR spectra of ·O2 - (a) and ·OH (d) under dark and UV-light irradiation for 15 min. Simulated adsorption processes of (b, c) O2 adsorption and (e, f) H2O adsorption on BiOCl nanosheets (b, e) and GQDs@BiOCl heterojunction (c, f). The calculated average bond lengths of O2 and H2O molecules during the adsorption processes are shown.

(a, b) Photocatalytic toluene degradation efficiency and mineralization efficiency of the BiOCl and GQDs@BiOCl nanosheet under UV light irradiation for 60 min. (c, d) Cycling tests of toluene degradation efficiency (c) and mineralization ratio (d) of the GQDs@BiOCl heterojunction for 300 min.
In situ DRIFTS spectra for toluene adsorption (a, b) and oxidation (c, d) processes on BiOCl nanosheet and GQDs@BiOCl heterojunction, respectively.
DFT calculations for adsorption energies (Eads) of reaction intermediates on photocatalysts.

head.ce:title: Efficient photocatalytic toluene degradation over heterojunction of GQDs@BiOCl ultrathin nanosheets with selective benzoic acid activation

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Research Center for Environmental and Energy Catalysis, Institute of Fundamental and Frontier Sciences, University of Electronic Science and Technology of China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Photocatalytic toluene degradation has attracted tremendous attention because of the growing environmental problem. However, conventional photocatalytic materials used for toluene degradation usually suffer from low carrier separation efficiency and poor stability which will degrade the catalytic performance. Herein, we report the synthesis of a novel heterostructure of GQDs@BiOCl ultrathin nanosheets where the GQDs can rapidly capture and transport photogenerated electrons for effective charge separation, promoting the generation of more reactive oxygen species (·O2 - and ·OH radicals) for toluene degradation. In situ DRIFTS measurement and theoretical calculation are performed to unveil the reaction intermediates and the underlying toluene oxidation mechanism. The GQDs@BiOCl heterojunction could facilitate the adsorption and conversion of toluene and the reaction intermediates. Especially, the heterojunction greatly enhances the activation and conversion of benzoic acid and thus expedites the complete toluene degradation. This work presents a new insight on the design of high-performance photocatalysts for efficient degradation of typical air pollutants.

ce:section.ce:para: Toluene, one of the hazardous volatile organic compounds (VOCs), is mainly emitted from industries such as petrochemicals, chemicals, pharmaceuticals, manufacturing, printing, painting and transportation activities (Huang et al., 2020; Lyu et al., 2020). Its exposure leads to the formation of secondary photochemical smog and particulate matter which could induce respiratory disease, carcinogenicity and reproductive toxicity. Thus, various technologies including adsorption (Deng et al., 2020; Zhao et al., 2018; Li et al., 2017), non-thermal plasma (Jiang et al., 2019; Xu et al., 2017), biofiltration (Vergara-Fernández et al., 2018; Kumar et al., 2019), catalytic combustion (Feng et al., 2019), and photocatalysis (Lai et al., 2019; Yang et al., 2021) have been developed to remove toluene in the past few years.

Among those, photocatalysis is considered as an innovative and promising approach to degrade toluene due to an inexhaustible supply of solar energy and its mild reaction conditions. In the past decade, a rapid development in homogeneous photocatalysts, such as β-Ga2O3 and SnO2 (Q. Chen et al., 2020; L. Chen et al., 2020; P. Chen et al., 2020; R. Chen et al., 2020), has been made towards the toluene degradation. Some strategies including doping, defect engineering, and crystallographic plane construction have also been explored to facilitate the removal of toluene (Jiang et al., 2020; L. Chen et al., 2020; P. Chen et al., 2020; R. Chen et al., 2020; Q. Chen et al., 2020; Li et al., 2020). However, the low efficiency of carrier separation limits the increased degradation rate for toluene oxidation. The key to improve the photocatalytic capability is to boost the charge separation of photogenerated carriers and restrain the subsequent recombination (Yu et al., 2014; Liao et al., 2020; Cao et al., 2018). A widely used method is to hybridize the conventional photocatalysts with other semiconductors to construct heterojunctions, in which the formed built-in electric field could accelerate the interfacial charge transfer and separation (Chen et al., 2016; Xing et al., 2021; Yuan et al., 2014; Shah et al., 2020). Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) show tunable band gap, high chemical stability, accessible surface chemistry and high electron extracting/transporting ability, which have been frequently utilized to create heterojunctions for enhanced carrier separation and transportation and thus to improve the photocatalytic performance (Li et al., 2015; Wang et al., 2016; Liu et al., 2020).

The process of toluene degradation is complicated and it usually needs a high activation energy to proceed the ring-opening reaction. The general process of photocatalytic toluene degradation consisted three main steps (Cao et al., 2000): (i) oxidation of toluene to benzyl alcohol, benzaldehyde and benzoic acid in sequence; (ii) aromatic ring-opening of benzyl alcohol, benzaldehyde and benzoic acid to form hydrocarbon chains; and (iii) the breakage of hydrocarbon chains to form CO2 and H2O. Among these steps, the ring-opening reaction with the highest activation energy is treated as the rate-determining step in the entire toluene degradation. Previous studies have shown that the enhanced selectivity of benzoic acid could promote the complete toluene degradation due to the smooth ring-opening pathway of benzoic acid (Q. Chen et al., 2020; P. Chen et al., 2020; R. Chen et al., 2020; L. Chen et al., 2020). It has been shown that BiOCl with exposed (010) facet displays a superior selectivity for the activation of benzoic acid, improving the photocatalytic decomposition of toluene (Li et al., 2019). Additionally, a graphene-hemin hybrid material shows an excellent catalytic performance in toluene oxidation due to its high selectivity towards benzoic acid (Li et al., 2013). Therefore, a promising strategy which could improve both the carrier separation and the selectivity of benzoic acid during activation process, needs to be explored to promote the complete toluene degradation.

In this work, a heterojunction of GQDs@BiOCl ultrathin nanosheet was developed as a high-performance photocatalyst for toluene degradation. Density functional theory (DFT) calculation and experimental characterizations were conducted to investigate the reaction mechanism of photocatalytic toluene degradation. In addition, the reaction intermediates and pathway of toluene decomposition were real-time monitored and recorded by in situ diffuse reflectance infrared Fourier transform spectroscopy (DRIFTS). The results suggested that the GQDs@BiOCl heterojunction could facilitate the charge separation and transportation, thus promoting the generation of active oxygen species (·O2 - and ·OH radicals) to oxidize toluene. More importantly, the decoration of GQDs upon BiOCl nanosheets elevated the activation and conversion of benzoic acid, and effectively boosted the complete toluene degradation. This work develops a novel GQDs@BiOCl heterojunction to accelerate the photocatalytic toluene degradation and presents a facile strategy to fabricate other ultrathin GQDs@nanomaterial heterojunctions for efficient air purification.

Graphene quantum dots were prepared via a typical hydrothermal reaction (Arunragsa et al., 2020; Wang et al., 2014). Firstly, 2 g of pyrene was added into concentrated HNO3 (160 mL) and then the mixture was reacted at 80 ℃ for 12 h under refluxing and stirring. After finishing the reaction, the brownish yellow suspension was washed with deionized water several times and dried in air at 60 ℃ overnight to collect the precursor (1,3,6-trinitropyrene). The precursor was added into 0.2 M NaOH aqueous solution and further transferred into a hydrothermal reactor which was heat-treated at 200 ℃ for 10 h. After the reactor cooling to room temperature, the GQDs solution was filtered through a 0.22 µm microporous membrane to remove insoluble carbon product, and further purified in a dialysis bag (retained molecular weight: 3000 Da) for 3 days, and finally freeze dried to obtain the dark brown GQDs. The overall yield of GQDs is about 60%.

The GQDs@BiOCl nanosheet heterojunction was fabricated via a one-pot hydrothermal method (Zhang et al., 2018; Guan et al., 2013). Briefly, 0.972 g of Bi(NO3)3.5H2O was dissolved in 50 mL of 0.1 M mannitol solution containing 0.8 g of PVP. After vigorous stirring for 10 mins, 10 mg of synthesized GQDs was added into above mixture and stirred for another 20 mins. Thereafter, 10 mL of NaCl solution (saturated) was added dropwise, forming a uniform white suspension. The mixture was then transferred to a 100 mL autoclave reactor and reacted at 160 ℃ for 3 h. After reaction, the resultant solid powder was washed with deionized water by centrifugation and freeze dried for further characterization. The pure BiOCl nanosheet was fabricated under the same condition without the addition of GQDs.

The morphologies and microstructures were characterized by field-emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM, Zeiss) and high-resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM). The specific surface area (BET) was measured by N2 adsorption-desorption instrument (ASAP 2020, USA). The crystallinity and composition were examined by power X-ray diffraction with Cu Kα radiation (XRD, Rigaku Co., Japan) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, ESCALAB250Xi). Raman spectroscopy (WITec, 532 nm wavelength) was used for the characterization of GQDs. Absorbance spectra were measured by UV–vis spectrometer (UV2550, Shimadzu). Steady and time-resolved fluorescence emission spectra were recorded by a fluorescence spectrophotometer. Electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) was investigated by a JES FA200 spectrometer. The EPR signal was spin-trapped by 5,5-dimethyl-1-pyrroline N-oxide (DMPO) which were dispersed in deionized water for DMPO-·OH and methanol for DMPO- ·O2 -, respectively.

The photocatalytic performance of the degradation of toluene was performed in a conventional continuous flow reaction system (1 L/min) which was shown in Scheme S1. The reaction chamber was made by a polymer glass and the lid was made of quartz. The toluene was fed into the reaction chamber from a compressed gas cylinder (500 ppm of toluene in N2) with a flow rate of 100 mL/min. The concentration of the toluene was diluted down to 50 ppm by feeding dry air (400 mL/min) and wet air (500 mL/min). The photocatalyst (0.4 g) was prepared onto four glass substrates by dispersing with ethanol for photocatalytic evaluation (around 0.1 g per glass substrate). During the reaction, a 300 W ultraviolet light (λ = 365 nm) was perpendicularly placed onto the reaction chamber. After reaching the equilibrium of adsorption/desorption, the light was switched on and a gas chromatography (GC 7890 Ⅱ, Techcomp Co. Ltd.) was used for real-time recording the concentration of toluene, H2O and CO2. The degradation ratio of toluene (η) was calculated using Eq. (1):
(1)
η (%) = (1-C/C
0 ) × 100%
where C
0 is the initial concentration of toluene, and C is downstream-side concentration of toluene after photocatalysis, respectively.

The mineralization ratio of toluene (M) was calculated using Eq. (2):
(2)
M
( % )
=
[
CO
2
]
produced
[
C
7
H
8
]
original
× 7
×
100 %

The toluene degradation was real-time monitored in a high-temperature chamber using a TENSOR II FTIR spectrometer (Bruker) equipped with an in situ diffuse reflectance cell (Harrick). Firstly, the photocatalysts were heat-treated in high-purity He at 300 ℃ to remove the adsorbed species. The background DRIFTS spectrum was recorded in the Ar atmosphere before the feeding of reactant gas. Subsequently, a mixing flow of toluene (25 mL/min) and O2 (25 mL/min) were fed into the reaction chamber to reach the adsorption/desorption equilibrium under dark condition. Thereafter, an UV light source (MUA-165) was used for the photocatalytic degradation of toluene (the reaction time was around 30 mins). During the reaction, the FT-IR spectrum was recorded every 4 min under the same condition. The scanning range in 4000–600 cm−1 of the IR spectra were recorded to analyze the toluene decomposition on the photocatalysts. A further normalization was performed on the original DRIFTS data to probe the evolution of reaction intermediates (species) over time.

DFT calculation was performed using the “Vienna ab initio simulation package” (VASP 5.4) together with the generalized gradient correlation functional (John et al., 1996; Kresse, 1996; Grimme, 2006). The framework of the projector-augment wave method was employed with a cut-off energy of 450 eV. The Gaussian smearing width was set as 0.2 eV (Joubert, 1999; Blochl, 1994). K points in the Brillouin zone were set to 3 × 3 × 1 within the Monkhorst Pack grid for the calculation of both structure optimization and electron-related features. The microstructure of BiOCl was constructed using a 5 × 4 supercell, and the GQDs presented with 7 benzene rings was built onto the BiOCl skeleton to form the GQDs@BiOCl structure. The surface was saturated by H atoms. All atoms (except the boundary atoms) were performed the geometry relaxation until the residual force was smaller than 0.05 eV Å−1. The adsorption energies (Eads) of reactants, reaction intermediates, H2O, and O2 molecules were calculated using the following equation:
(3)
E
ads
=
E
tot
− (
E
mol
+
E
cat
)
where E
tot , represents the total energy of the adsorption system, E
mol is the energy of the adsorbed molecules, and E
cat represents the energy of the photocatalyst, respectively.

The morphology and microstructure of as-prepared BiOCl and GQDs@BiOCl samples were characterized and analyzed by SEM and TEM ( Figs. 1a-b and 2). Both of them exhibit the 2D nanosheet shape with the size in range of 30–120 nm (Fig. 1a and b). The thickness of the nanosheet is about 2–5 nm. Apparently, the addition of GQDs contributes to the reduced lateral size and better dispersion (Fig. 2a and e). The BET surface areas of bare BiOCl and GQDs@BiOCl are 14.3 and 15.2 m2 g−1, respectively (Fig. S1). Selected area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern suggests the tetragonal phase of the obtained BiOCl (Fig. 2b). The HRTEM image in Fig. 2c and d displays the lattice fringe of the BiOCl nanosheet, which can be assigned to the (110) plane with an interplanar spacing of 0.276 nm. Compared with the pure BiOCl nanosheet, large quantity of GQDs with size of around 2.8 nm was observed in GQDs@BiOCl nanosheet heterojunction (Fig. 2f), revealing the successful decoration of the GQDs. Moreover, the lattice spacing of GQDs is 0.21 nm, corresponding to its (100) plane (Fig. 2g and h). The size and crystal structure of these GQDs in the GQDs@BiOCl heterojunction are in agreement with that of the pure GQDs (Fig. S2), indicating the high stability of GQDs. The Raman spectrum of GQDs is shown in Fig. S2d. Meanwhile, the EDS mapping of the pure BiOCl and GQDs@BiOCl nanosheet demonstrates the uniform distribution of Bi, Cl, O, and C (Figs. S3 and S4).

XRD patterns of BiOCl and GQDs@BiOCl nanosheets suggest the formation of tetragonal phase of BiOCl (JCPDS No. 06–0249, Fig. 1c). Diffraction peaks of the BiOCl at 11.98°, 25.86°, 32.50°, 33.45°, 46.64°, and 54.09° are assigned to the planes of (0 0 1), (1 0 1), (1 1 0), (1 0 2), (2 0 0), (2 1 1). Despite the characteristic pattern of a (002) plane is observed in pure GQDs (Fig. S5), no diffraction peak of GQDs was observed in the GQDs@BiOCl heterojunction due to its trace amount (Di et al., 2015). The composition and chemical state were analyzed by XPS measurements (Fig. 1d−f). The survey spectrum shows the characteristic peaks of Bi 4f, Cl 2p, C 1s and O 1s (Fig. 1d). The Bi 4f spectrum of the BiOCl and GQDs@BiOCl nanosheet can be deconvolved into two doublets, i.e., 4f7/2 and 4f5/2 of Bi3+ photoelectron emission with a splitting energy of 5.3 eV. The Bi 4f7/2 and 4f5/2 peaks of the BiOCl located at 159.1 eV and 164.4 eV display a 0.2 eV shift towards lower binding energy in the GQDs@BiOCl heterojunction (Fig. 1e). The Cl 2p in the GQDs@BiOCl heterojunction also reveals a shift towards the lower binding energy (Fig. S6). This binding energy shift of Bi 4f and Cl 2p could be due to the interaction between the GQDs and BiOCl and the induced oxygen vacancies in the heterojunction (Qiu et al., 2020; Liu et al., 2016; Zhang et al., 2019). The oxygen vacancies would cause the excessive electrons for Bi and Cl, resulting in the decreased binding energies of Bi 4f and Cl 2p XPS spectra. The XPS peaks locating at 284.8, 285.8 and 287.9 eV are assigned to the C-C, C-O-C, O-CO, respectively (Fig. 1f). The intensity ratio of C-C/C-O is 2.50 in the GQDs@BiOCl nanosheet heterojunction which is much higher than the value of 1.49 in the pure BiOCl nanosheet due to the plentiful C-C skeleton in GQDs. These results demonstrated the successful preparation of GQDs@BiOCl heterojunction. The electron localization function (ELF) was performed to investigate the chemical interactions between GQDs and BiOCl (Fig. S7). There was no apparent chemical interaction between them. It would be the Van der Waals force that occurs at the interface between GQDs and BiOCl.

Fig. 3a displays the UV–vis DRS spectra of the BiOCl and GQDs@BiOCl nanosheet in range of 200–800 nm. It’s apparent that the ultrathin BiOCl nanosheets show a strong absorption in ultraviolet region with an absorption edge at ~375 nm, corresponding to a bandgap energy of 3.3 eV (Fig. 3b). After decoration with GQDs, the absorbance exhibits an obvious increase in visible region, on account of the intrinsic absorption of GQDs (Fig. S8). The GQDs@BiOCl nanosheet heterojunction reveals a considerable absorption in the UV–visible spectra. Besides, the photoluminescence spectra (PL) of the BiOCl and GQDs@BiOCl nanosheet were characterized to study their carrier recombination properties (Fig. 3c). Both of them manifest a broad emission peak in the range of 400–600 nm, but the PL intensity of the GQDs@BiOCl heterojunction is much lower than that of pure BiOCl, demonstrating an enhanced carrier separation capacity in the heterojunction. The time-resolved fluorescence decay spectra were further measured to study the carrier motion dynamics (Fig. 3d, Table S1). The lifetime of carriers in the GQDs@BiOCl heterojunction (6.492 ns) was increased as compared to pure BiOCl nanosheet (5.255 ns), indicating that the GQDs can significantly enhance the carrier separation and transportation efficiency. These carriers will effectively activate the reactants of O2 and H2O to generate reactive oxygen species (Dong et al., 2019). In addition, the electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) spectra indicate a distinct electron delocalization phenomenon in the GQDs@BiOCl nanosheet heterojunction under dark and light conditions ( Figs. 4a and S9). The charge density distribution of the GQDs@BiOCl heterojunction shows the transfer of these electrons from BiOCl to GQDs (Fig. 4b). Therefore, GQDs, as excellent electronic conductors, can effectively capture and transfer photoelectrons from BiOCl to enhance the carrier separation and transportation to suppress the carrier recombination, prolonging the lifetime of the carriers, which is in good agreement with the result of time-resolved fluorescence decay spectra.

The photogenerated carriers were kinetically separated and transferred to react with the adsorbed O2 and H2O molecules. O2 molecules were reduced by the photoelectrons to generate superoxide radicals (·O2 -), while the hydroxyl radicals (·OH) were formed via the oxidation of H2O molecules by photogenerated holes (Q. Chen et al., 2020; P. Chen et al., 2020; R. Chen et al., 2020; L. Chen et al., 2020; Li et al., 2018b, 2018a). To elucidate the reactive species of the BiOCl and GQDs@BiOCl nanosheet for toluene oxidation, the EPR spectra were recorded with different irradiation time (0, 5, and 15 min) using two spin trapping agents. It was shown that ·O2 - and ·OH radicals were detected in the presence of DMPO in methanol and aqueous solution. It can be seen that the peaks of ·O2 - and ·OH radicals appeared immediately after irradiation for the BiOCl and GQDs@BiOCl nanosheet and the peak intensities increase with prolonging the irradiation time. Besides, the GQDs@BiOCl heterojunction shows a much stronger peak intensity for the increased generation of ·O2 - and ·OH radicals ( Fig. 5a and d), leading to an enhanced oxidation capacity. In the meantime, the adsorption and activation of O2 and H2O molecules to produce ·O2 - and ·OH radicals were further verified by DFT calculations. The adsorption energies of O2 molecules were −0.699 and −0.786 eV (Fig. 5b-c), while those of H2O molecules were −0.747 and −1.870 eV on the BiOCl and GQDs@BiOCl nanosheet, respectively (Fig. 5e−f). Evidently, the GQDs@BiOCl nanosheet heterojunction promotes the adsorption of O2 and H2O molecules with stronger adsorption energies. With regard to the activation, the bond lengths of OO and H-O for O2 and H2O molecules adsorbed on the GQDs@BiOCl heterojunction were elongated to 1.274 and 1.014 Å, showing a much more elongation than that on pure BiOCl nanosheet. Therefore, the O2 and H2O molecules are more easily adsorbed and activated to generate ·O2 - and ·OH radicals on the surface of the GQDs@BiOCl heterojunction. The enhanced active radicals can result in the promotion of the ring-opening reaction of toluene and its intermediates and thus leads to an elevated degradation of toluene.

The photocatalytic performance of toluene degradation on the BiOCl and GQDs@BiOCl nanosheet was characterized under UV irradiation ( Figs. 6 and S10). As shown in Fig. 6a, the removal ratio of toluene on the GQDs@BiOCl nanosheet heterojunction is of 65.4%, which shows a 104% enhancement as compared to that on pure BiOCl substrate (32.1%) (Li et al., 2019; Gao et al., 2012). The mineralization efficiency, corresponding to the CO2 production, was determined to be 65.2% and 29.3% for the GQDs@BiOCl and BiOCl nanosheet, respectively (Fig. 6b). Furthermore, the high efficiency of toluene degradation and mineralization can be maintained without noticeable decay for the GQDs@BiOCl heterojunction (Fig. 6c-d). The morphology and phase (composition) are also well maintained after the cycling test, indicating a superior stability of the GQDs@BiOCl heterojunction (Fig. S11). The photocatalytic toluene degradation under xenon lamp irradiation was investigated as well (Fig. S12). Both pure BiOCl and GQDs@BiOCl heterojunction do not exhibit apparent visible-light activity of toluene removal. Thus, this highly enhanced photocatalytic activity and stability of toluene degradation and mineralization on the GQDs@BiOCl heterojunction can be ascribed to the elevated charge separation and transportation of photogenerated carriers through GQDs, which could induce more reactive oxygen species (·O2 - and ·OH radicals) to accelerate the oxidation of toluene and its intermediates to form benzoic acid and further facilitate the ring-opening reaction to decompose the pollutant toluene. Besides, the reactive species were also proved by introduction the scavengers. A certain amount of tert-butyl alcohol (TBA) and p-benzoquinone (PBQ) were added (the ratio is around 1:100 to the sample) to act as scavengers to remove hydroxyl radicals (·OH) and superoxide radicals (·O2 -) respectively. As shown in Fig. S13, the removal ratios of toluene were reduced to 28.7% and 25.2% when adding TBA and PBQ. Therefore, the hydroxyl and superoxide radicals are the reactive species and playing important roles in the degradation of toluene.

To further unveil the reaction mechanism and conversion pathway of toluene degradation on the BiOCl and GQDs@BiOCl nanosheet, the adsorbed reactants and reaction intermediates were real time monitored using in situ DRIFTS. For the adsorption on pure BiOCl, the characteristic absorption peak of toluene (1327 and 2933 cm−1) (Li et al., 2019; Zhu et al., 2015) appeared when the toluene was injected under dark conditions ( Fig. 7a). Simultaneously, the absorption bands of benzyl alcohol (3239, 3459, and 3580 cm−1) (Chen et al., 2010), benzaldehyde (1637, 1558 and 1536 cm−1) (Wang et al., 2019), and benzoic acid (1377 and 1457 cm−1) (Dong et al., 2019) were observed, indicating that the toluene has been partially oxidized spontaneously. For the GQDs@BiOCl nanosheet heterojunction, the typical DRIFTS absorption peaks of toluene (1306 and 2937 cm−1), benzyl alcohol (3269, 3450, and 3580 cm−1), benzaldehyde (1625 and 1600 cm−1) and benzoic acid (1450 cm−1) were also assigned (Fig. 7b). The increased peak intensity of each absorption band reveals the gradual accumulation of toluene and reaction intermediates over time. It’s worth noting that the adsorption of toluene, benzaldehyde and benzoic acid shows a much faster equilibrium on the GQDs@BiOCl nanosheet heterojunction as compared to that on pure BiOCl, proving the enhanced adsorption on the heterojunction. The adsorption energy of toluene and its intermediates were analyzed by DFT calculations (Table S2). The positive value of Eads represents an endothermic process, while the negative value means the exothermic reaction. In general, the larger absolute value of Eads refers to the stronger interaction between the adsorbate and the utilized photocatalyst. As shown in Fig. 8, the adsorption energies of toluene, benzyl alcohol, benzaldehyde and benzoic acid on the BiOCl and GQDs@BiOCl nanosheet were negative, indicating the exothermic adsorption process. In addition, the adsorption energies of toluene, benzyl alcohol, benzaldehyde and benzoic acid were −0.57, −2.00, −1.83 and −0.98 eV on pure BiOCl nanosheet, while those were −0.65, −1.99, −1.86 and −2.19 eV on GQDs@BiOCl heterojunction. The increased adsorption energies indicated the promoted adsorption and stronger interaction between the adsorbate and GQDs@BiOCl heterojunction, which was consistent with the DRIFTS results. Especially, the significantly enhanced adsorption and activation of benzoic acid on the GQDs@BiOCl heterojunction were obtained. On the one hand, the abundant reactive oxygen species (·O2 - and ·OH radicals) generated on the GQDs@BiOCl nanosheet heterojunction accelerate the complete oxidation of toluene step by step to form benzoic acid and thus promote the selective formation and activation of benzoic acid. On the other hand, the smooth adsorption and activation pathway from toluene to benzoic acid and the greatest adsorption energy of benzoic acid on the GQDs@BiOCl nanosheet heterojunction guarantee its high selective activation of benzoic acid. In consequence, the GQDs@BiOCl heterojunction promotes the high selective adsorption and activation of benzoic acid, which could facilitate its selective conversion and thus expedite the ring-opening process to achieve the complete toluene degradation.

Subsequently, the toluene and its intermediates were continuously monitored under UV light irradiation to investigate the corresponding reaction pathway of the toluene decomposition. Fig. 7c displays that the characteristic peak intensities of benzyl alcohol (3246, 3459, and 3580 cm−1) and benzaldehyde (1625 and 1558 cm−1) gradually increase, while that of benzoic acid (1457 and 1374 cm−1) firstly increase and then decrease on the BiOCl nanosheet after irradiation for 30 min. With regard to the GQDs@BiOCl nanosheet heterojunction, the peak intensities of benzyl alcohol (3268, 3450, and 3580 cm−1) increase, and that of benzaldehyde (1620 cm−1) and benzoic acid (1446 cm−1) decrease with the time (Fig. 7d). It’s worth noting that the GQDs@BiOCl nanosheet heterojunction facilitates the ring-opening and conversion of benzaldehyde and benzoic acid compared with the pure BiOCl nanosheet. In particular, the decrease of benzoic acid is more significant than other reaction intermediates on the GQDs@BiOCl heterojunction, indicating its higher selective conversion of benzoic acid. This high selective conversion of benzoic acid on the GQDs@BiOCl heterojunction from in situ DRIFTS is consistent with the selective activation of benzoic acid from the adsorption energy calculation by DFT. Therefore, the decoration of GQDs on the ultrathin BiOCl nanosheet improves the carrier separation to generate more reactive oxygen species, which could further oxide toluene and its intermediate to form benzoic acid. On the other side, the GQDs@BiOCl heterojunction shows a stronger adsorption and interaction with benzoic acid to effectively promote its selective activation conversion and thus accelerates the complete toluene degradation. The high-efficiency and stable toluene decomposition are achieved on the GQDs@BiOCl nanosheet heterojunction.

In summary, the ultrathin GQDs@BiOCl nanosheet heterojunction has been synthesized and applied for photocatalytic toluene degradation. DFT calculation and experimental characterization were performed to investigate the detailed degradation mechanism. The results show that the GQDs@BiOCl heterojunction could facilitate the charge separation and transportation of photogenerated carriers and thus boost the activation of O2 and H2O molecules to produce ·O2 - and ·OH radicals when compared with the pure BiOCl. In addition, the decoration of GQDs on the BiOCl nanosheet improves the adsorption and activation of toluene and its reaction intermediates. Particularly, the heterojunction effectively enhances the selective conversion and ring-opening of benzoic acid and hence promotes the entire toluene degradation. This work develops an effective and stable photocatalyst via the decoration of GQDs onto BiOCl and demonstrates a high photocatalytic performance in toluene decomposition. The proposed strategy in this work could further guide the fabrication and study of other ultrathin GQDs/nanomaterial heterojunctions for the efficient and safe removal of air pollutants.

Xiaoli Zhao: Data curation, Formal analysis, Methodology, Visualization, Writing - original draft. Bangwei Deng: Formal analysis, Methodology. Fei Li: Data curation, Methodology. Ming Huang: Data curation, Methodology. Yanjuan Sun: Data curation, Methodology. Jieyuan Li: Writing - review & editing. Fan Dong: Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Supervision, Writing - review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant Nos. 21822601, 22006009), Sichuan Natural Science Foundation for Distinguished Young Scholars (2021JDJQ0006), and the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (ZYGX2019Z021), the 111 Project (B20030).

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y.-P. Yuan, L.-W. Ruan, J. Barber, S.C. Joachim Loo, C. Xue, Hetero-nanostructured suspended photocatalysts for solar-to-fuel conversion, Energy Environ. Sci. 7 (2014) 3934‐3951.

ce:bib-reference.ce:label: Yu et al., 2014

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Hot-electron-transfer enhancement for the efficient energy conversion of visible light

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Yu, Y.H. Kim, S.Y. Lee, H.D. Song, J. Yi, Hot-electron-transfer enhancement for the efficient energy conversion of visible light, Angew. Chem. Int. Ed. 53 (2014) 11203‐11207.

ce:bib-reference.ce:label: Zhang et al., 2019

sb:title.sb:maintitle: In situ surfactant-free synthesis of ultrathin BiOCl/g-C3N4 nanosheets for enhanced visible-light photodegradation of rhodamine B

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X. Zhang, D. An, D. Feng, F. Liang, Z. Chen, W. Liu, Z. Yang, M. Xian, In situ surfactant-free synthesis of ultrathin BiOCl/g-C3N4 nanosheets for enhanced visible-light photodegradation of rhodamine B, Appl. Surf. Sci. 476 (2019) 706‐715.

ce:bib-reference.ce:label: Zhang et al., 2018

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Ultrathin BiOCl single-crystalline nanosheets with large reactive facets area and high electron mobility efficiency: a superior candidate for high-performance dye self-photosensitization photocatalytic fuel cell

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: L. Zhang, C.-G. Niu, X.-F. Zhao, C. Liang, H. Guo, G.-M. Zeng, Ultrathin BiOCl Single-Crystalline Nanosheets with Large Reactive Facets Area and High Electron Mobility Efficiency: A Superior Candidate for High-Performance Dye Self-Photosensitization Photocatalytic Fuel Cell, ACS Appl. Mater. Interfaces 10 (2018) 39723‐39734.

ce:bib-reference.ce:label: Zhao et al., 2018

sb:title.sb:maintitle: An experimental and theoretical study of the adsorption removal of toluene and chlorobenzene on coconut shell derived carbon

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X. Zhao, X. Zeng, Y. Qin, X. Li, T. Zhu, X. Tang, An experimental and theoretical study of the adsorption removal of toluene and chlorobenzene on coconut shell derived carbon, Chemosphere, 206 (2018) 285‐292.

ce:bib-reference.ce:label: Zhu et al., 2015

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Fabricate and characterization of Ag/BaAl2O4 and its photocatalytic performance towards oxidation of gaseous toluene studied by FTIR spectroscopy

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Z. Zhu, F. Liu, W. Zhang, Fabricate and characterization of Ag/BaAl2O4 and its photocatalytic performance towards oxidation of gaseous toluene studied by FTIR spectroscopy, Mater. Res. Bull. 64 (2015) 68‐75.
